events.title: President Jovenel Moïse Assassinated
events.detail: President Jovenel Moïse, 53, was shot 12 times by a 28-man mercenary team—mostly Colombian nationals—at his private residence in Pelerin 5, Port-au-Prince. First Lady Martine Moïse was critically wounded and airlifted to a Miami hospital. The assassination created an immediate power vacuum and set off a spiral of state collapse.

events.title: Power Struggle: Claude Joseph vs. Ariel Henry
events.detail: Interim PM Claude Joseph declared a state of siege and claimed executive authority following the assassination. However, Ariel Henry—who had been designated PM by Moïse just two days before his death but was never sworn in—also claimed legitimacy. Two parallel power centers emerged, deepening institutional paralysis.

events.title: CORE Group Backs Ariel Henry
events.detail: The international CORE Group—comprising ambassadors from the US, France, Germany, Brazil, Canada, Spain, the EU, the UN, and the OAS—issued a statement backing Ariel Henry as designated prime minister over interim PM Claude Joseph. Critics, including former US envoy Daniel Foote, condemned the decision as undemocratic meddling that legitimized an unelected official.

events.title: Ariel Henry Sworn In as Prime Minister
events.detail: Ariel Henry, a 71-year-old neurosurgeon, was formally sworn in as Haiti's prime minister following Claude Joseph's resignation the previous day. Henry assumed office without having been elected, backed by international diplomatic pressure rather than a popular mandate. His government's legitimacy was contested from the outset by opposition coalitions and civil society.

events.title: G9 Gang Federation Expands Control in Port-au-Prince
events.detail: With the presidential power vacuum, the G9 an Fanmi e Alye federation led by Jimmy 'Barbecue' Chérizier rapidly expanded territorial control across Port-au-Prince neighborhoods. The gang used snipers, kidnapping, sexual violence, and arson to consolidate zones including Cité Soleil, Bel Air, La Saline, and Martissant. Civilians faced systematic extortion and displacement.

events.title: 7.2 Magnitude Earthquake Strikes Southern Haiti
events.detail: A 7.2 magnitude earthquake struck the Tiburon Peninsula in southern Haiti, killing over 2,200 people and injuring more than 12,200. The towns of Les Cayes, Jérémie, and surrounding areas suffered massive destruction. The disaster compounded Haiti's political crisis and overwhelmed emergency services already strained by gang violence in the capital.

events.title: Montana Accord Opposition Coalition Forms
events.detail: Civic, religious, and political organizations gathered at the Hotel Montana in Pétionville to form an opposition coalition. The Montana Accord proposed a two-year transitional government headed by civil society figures rather than political parties. The coalition represented the most organized alternative to Henry's government and called for elections as soon as security permitted.

events.title: Henry Signs Limited Political Accord
events.detail: PM Ariel Henry signed a political accord with several opposition parties and civil society groups on the 20th anniversary of the 9/11 attacks. The accord outlined a path to elections but was rejected by the Montana Accord coalition as insufficient. It failed to produce a stable governance framework and left Haiti with multiple competing political factions.

events.title: G9 Blockades Varreux Fuel Terminal
events.detail: Jimmy 'Barbecue' Chérizier and his G9 federation blockaded the Varreux fuel terminal, which supplies approximately 70% of Haiti's petroleum. Chérizier demanded PM Henry's resignation, stating 'If Ariel Henry resigns at 8:00, then at 8:05, we remove all the barricades.' Hospitals, generators, and water pumping systems faced critical shortages lasting nearly a month.

events.title: Fuel Blockade Partially Lifted After Weeks
events.detail: After nearly a month of critical fuel shortages, Chérizier temporarily relented and fuel trucks were allowed to resume operations at Varreux. The UN Security Council had adopted a resolution calling for an immediate end to organized criminal activity blocking fuel supplies. The resolution passed but did not address underlying governance failures that enabled gang power.

events.title: Gang Violence Severs Martissant Corridor
events.detail: Armed gangs effectively sealed off the Martissant neighborhood, blocking the Route Nationale 2 highway connecting Port-au-Prince to Haiti's southern peninsula. The blockade cut off humanitarian access to hundreds of thousands of people and forced MSF to suspend operations at its Martissant emergency hospital due to sniper fire and gang incursions.

events.title: Kidnapping Wave Terrorizes Capital
events.detail: Gang-organized kidnappings surged dramatically in early 2022, targeting businesspeople, clergy, schoolchildren, and aid workers. Ransom demands ranged from a few hundred to tens of thousands of dollars. The RNDDH and ACLED documented hundreds of cases, many involving torture and sexual violence. Police were frequently unable or unwilling to respond.

events.title: Cité Soleil Massacre: G9 vs. G-Pèp War
events.detail: On the first anniversary of Moïse's assassination, war erupted between the G9 federation (led by Chérizier) and the rival G-Pèp coalition (led by Gabriel Jean Pierre/Ti Gabriel) for control of Cité Soleil. At least 207 civilians were confirmed killed by UN investigators—including by G9 snipers who fired indiscriminately—with 57 documented gang rapes and thousands displaced. The UN described it as one of Haiti's worst massacres.

events.title: G9 Seizes Full Control of Varreux Terminal—Second Blockade
events.detail: The G9 federation seized complete control of the Varreux fuel terminal, cutting off an estimated 10 million gallons of diesel, gasoline, and 800,000 gallons of kerosene. Hospitals began shutting down or reducing operations. When Henry formally requested foreign military assistance to end the crisis, Chérizier shifted demands to seeking amnesty for active arrest warrants against himself and other gang leaders.

events.title: Henry Formally Requests International Armed Force
events.detail: PM Ariel Henry wrote formally to the UN Secretary-General requesting 'the immediate deployment of a specialized armed force' to end the fuel blockade and restore security. The letter triggered months of UN Security Council deliberations. Canada and the US expressed skepticism about a new mission without a willing lead nation, while Henry's legitimacy to make such a request was challenged domestically.

events.title: UN Resolution 2653: Haiti Sanctions Regime Established
events.detail: The UN Security Council unanimously adopted Resolution 2653, establishing the first comprehensive sanctions regime targeting Haiti since 2001. It imposed travel bans, asset freezes, and an arms embargo on individuals responsible for gang violence, including Jimmy Chérizier. A dedicated UN Sanctions Committee was created. Critics argued the measures were too limited to change facts on the ground.

events.title: Cholera Returns After 3-Year Absence
events.detail: Haiti detected a new cholera outbreak in October 2022, ending a three-year absence of the disease. Nearly 87,000 suspected cases and over 1,700 deaths were recorded within months. The outbreak was concentrated in gang-controlled areas where water and sanitation infrastructure had collapsed, and in displacement camps. MSF and PAHO warned the epidemic would spread given the breakdown of public health systems.

events.title: Mass Protests Demand Henry's Resignation
events.detail: Tens of thousands of Haitians took to the streets in September and October 2022, demanding PM Ariel Henry's resignation amid fuel shortages, gang violence, and accusations of corruption. Protesters in Port-au-Prince, Cap-Haïtien, and other cities chanted 'Henry must go.' The PetroCaribe scandal—allegations that billions in Venezuelan oil proceeds were stolen under previous governments—fueled popular anger.

events.title: 160,000 Haitians Displaced by Gang Violence
events.detail: The UN reported at least 160,000 people had been internally displaced since mid-2021, driven primarily by gang violence in and around Port-au-Prince. Displacement camps in Tabarre, Delmas, and other areas were severely overcrowded with minimal sanitation. The IDP figure masked far larger 'hidden displacement' as families moved in with relatives across the country.

events.title: Homicides More Than Double: 4,789 Killed
events.detail: The UN recorded 4,789 people murdered in Haiti in 2023—a 119% increase over 2022—with an additional 1,698 injured and 2,490 kidnapped. The homicide rate reached 40.9 per 100,000. BINUH noted that gang violence had become the leading cause of death for men aged 18–59 in the metropolitan area. Researchers estimated the true death toll was underreported by at least 70%.

events.title: Gangs Control ~80% of Port-au-Prince
events.detail: By mid-2023, armed gangs effectively controlled approximately 80% of Port-au-Prince and were expanding into secondary cities and rural departments. The National Police (PNH) was outgunned, understaffed, and riddled with informants. Key arteries including Routes Nationales 1, 2, and 3 were subject to gang taxation, ambushes, and occasional full closures.

events.title: Viv Ansanm: Historic G9 / G-Pèp Alliance Announced
events.detail: Former rival gang coalitions G9 an Fanmi e Alye and G-Pèp formally announced the Viv Ansanm ('Living Together') alliance on September 22, 2023. The union united two groups that had fought a bloody war in Cité Soleil just 15 months earlier. Chérizier presented the alliance as a move toward protecting civilians, but analysts warned it dramatically concentrated criminal power and foreshadowed a coordinated offensive against the state.

events.title: UN Security Council Authorizes MSS Mission (Resolution 2699)
events.detail: The UN Security Council adopted Resolution 2699 by 13–0, with China and Russia abstaining, authorizing a Multinational Security Support Mission in Haiti for an initial 12-month period. The resolution granted the MSS temporary executive policing authority including powers of detention and arrest. Kenya emerged as the lead nation after initially facing a domestic legal challenge. The target force size was 2,500 personnel.

events.title: Kenya Parliamentary Approval to Lead MSS
events.detail: Kenya's National Security Council and Cabinet approved the Haiti deployment on October 13, 2023, and a joint session of the National Assembly and Senate ratified the dispatch of 1,000 National Police Service officers on November 16, 2023. The deployment was briefly paused after Kenya's High Court ruled a bilateral agreement with Haiti was required. A reciprocal agreement was signed in March 2024.

events.title: Kidnappings Surge 83% Over 2022
events.detail: Haiti recorded 2,490 kidnappings in 2023, representing an 83% increase over 2022. Gang factions used kidnappings as a primary revenue source, targeting business owners, health workers, clergy, and school staff. Mass kidnappings—where groups of passengers were abducted from buses or vehicles—became increasingly common along key routes. The PNH anti-kidnapping unit was overwhelmed and underfunded.

events.title: Parliamentary Mandate Expires; Haiti Left with No Elected Officials
events.detail: On January 9, 2023, the mandates of the last remaining elected Haitian legislators expired, leaving PM Henry as the sole governing authority with no legislature, no elected president, and no municipal officials. Haiti became a country governed entirely by a single unelected individual backed by international diplomatic recognition. Opposition coalitions called it a constitutional rupture requiring urgent political resolution.

events.title: 5.5 Million Haitians Face Acute Food Insecurity
events.detail: WFP and IPC assessments found 5.5 million people—nearly half the population—in acute food insecurity in Haiti in 2023. Over 115,000 were in IPC Phase 5 (catastrophe), the most severe category. Gang blockades of humanitarian corridors, fuel shortages hampering food distribution, and displacement of farming communities all contributed. The WFP warned of a looming famine in gang-controlled urban areas.

events.title: Viv Ansanm Launches Coordinated Capital Offensive
events.detail: While PM Ariel Henry was abroad signing the MSS bilateral agreement with Kenya, Viv Ansanm launched a coordinated offensive targeting Port-au-Prince infrastructure on February 29. Gunfire erupted near the main international airport. Police stations in Pétion-Ville and other neighborhoods were seized. The offensive appeared timed to prevent Henry from returning and demonstrated the gang coalition's strategic coordination.

events.title: Coordinated Prison Breaks Free 4,700 Inmates
events.detail: Gang forces stormed the Croix-des-Bouquets National Penitentiary and the Civil Prison of Port-au-Prince in coordinated attacks on March 2–3, freeing approximately 4,700 inmates. The mass escapes included convicted murderers, gang members, and political prisoners. The prison director and several guards were killed. The jailbreaks further overwhelmed an already collapsed justice system.

events.title: Airport Assaulted, International Flights Suspended
events.detail: Armed gangs attacked Toussaint Louverture International Airport, forcing the permanent closure of Haiti's main international airport to commercial traffic. PM Henry, stranded in Puerto Rico, was unable to return to Haiti. American Airlines, Air France, and other carriers suspended all flights. The closure isolated Haiti from international aid, trade, and communications.

events.title: Ariel Henry Resigns, Effective April 24
events.detail: Following a CARICOM-brokered emergency meeting in Kingston, Henry announced his resignation on March 11, 2024, while stranded outside the country. He remained in a diplomatic limbo for weeks before formally signing his resignation letter from Los Angeles on April 24. He had governed for nearly three years without democratic legitimacy, and his departure opened the path for a transitional council.

events.title: Transitional Presidential Council (TPC) Sworn In
events.detail: The nine-member Transitional Presidential Council was sworn in at Haiti's National Palace on April 25, 2024. The council—comprising seven voting representatives from political blocs and the private sector, plus two non-voting civil society and religious figures—was mandated to exercise presidential powers until an elected president is inaugurated or February 7, 2026. The TPC was seen as a pragmatic compromise brokered by CARICOM and the United States.

events.title: Garry Conille Appointed Prime Minister
events.detail: The Transitional Presidential Council selected Garry Conille as prime minister on May 28, 2024, by a 6-to-1 vote. Conille, 52, was a former UN special envoy and had previously served briefly as PM in 2011–12. His technocratic background and international connections made him a consensus candidate. He was sworn in on June 2, 2024, but would face an impossible security and governance environment.

events.title: First 400 Kenyan MSS Officers Arrive in Haiti
events.detail: The first contingent of 400 Kenyan National Police Service officers landed in Port-au-Prince on June 25, 2024, nearly eight months after the UN authorized the MSS mission. The deployment was far below the authorized 2,500 force—Kenya had pledged 1,000—due to funding gaps, equipment shortfalls, and diplomatic delays. The officers were welcomed by Haitian officials and initially focused on securing key government buildings.

events.title: Additional 200 Kenyan Officers Deployed
events.detail: A second wave of 200 Kenyan police officers arrived in July 2024, bringing the total Kenyan contingent to approximately 600 and the overall MSS force to around 800 personnel from Kenya, Jamaica, Bahamas, and other CARICOM nations. The force remained well below the 2,500 target. Funding was a persistent constraint, with the mission receiving only about 2/3 of its $600 million annual operational budget.

events.title: Pont-Sondé Massacre: 115+ Killed by Gran Grif Gang
events.detail: The Gran Grif gang, led by Luckson Elan, attacked the town of Pont-Sondé in the Artibonite Department, killing at least 115 people—including babies, elderly women, and entire families—in one of Haiti's deadliest single gang attacks. Gunmen arrived by canoe to prevent escape. Over 6,270 people fled the area. The attack appeared to be retaliation against residents accused of supporting a rival anti-gang coalition. Government security forces arrived more than 24 hours late.

events.title: Conille Ousted; Alix Didier Fils-Aimé Named PM
events.detail: The Transitional Presidential Council removed Garry Conille after just five months and appointed businessman Alix Didier Fils-Aimé as Prime Minister on November 10, 2024. Fils-Aimé, 52, holds degrees from Boston University and Harvard, made his fortune in telecommunications and service industries, and was backed by US and private-sector interests. The replacement highlighted the TPC's internal divisions and short governance horizon.

events.title: Wharf Jérémie Massacre: 207 Confirmed Dead
events.detail: The gang controlling Wharf Jérémie in Cité Soleil, led by Micanor Altes ('King Micanor'), systematically executed 207 confirmed civilians over five days in December 2024. The gang leader accused residents—including elderly Vodou practitioners—of casting a spell on his son who had died. Victims were dragged from homes and places of worship, shot or hacked with machetes, and their bodies burned and thrown into the sea. The UN described it as an atrocity.

events.title: 2024 Closes with 5,600+ Killed in Gang Violence
events.detail: OHCHR documented over 5,600 people killed in gang violence in Haiti in 2024—a 20% increase over 2023—with over 2,200 injured and 1.3 million people internally displaced. Gender-based violence reached 4,487 reported cases in the first seven months alone, a 40% increase. By year's end, gangs controlled approximately 85% of Port-au-Prince and were expanding into Artibonite and Centre departments.

events.title: First MSS Casualty: Kenyan Officer Killed
events.detail: Kenyan police officer Samuel Tompei Kaetuai became the first MSS fatality, killed in a gunfight with gang members in Port-au-Prince on February 24, 2025. The death underscored the mission's operational difficulties: gangs possessed assault rifles, heavy weapons, and armored vehicles, in many cases outgunning the MSS. The mission had by this point deployed approximately 989 personnel, less than 40% of the authorized strength.

events.title: Fritz Alphonse Jean Becomes TPC President
events.detail: Fritz Alphonse Jean, representing the Montana Accord, was sworn in as President of the Transitional Presidential Council on March 7, 2025, succeeding Leslie Voltaire in the rotating chairmanship. Jean, an economist and former Haitian Central Bank governor, was scheduled to lead the TPC until August 7, 2025. His appointment was seen as a concession to the civil society opposition that had long challenged the TPC's legitimacy.

events.title: US Designates Viv Ansanm as Foreign Terrorist Organization
events.detail: The US State Department formally designated Viv Ansanm as a Foreign Terrorist Organization (FTO) on May 2, 2025, making it unlawful for US persons to provide material support to the coalition. The designation enabled asset freezes, travel bans, and potential prosecution of international financiers. Chérizier called the designation 'politically motivated.' The action followed the Trump administration's classification of Haitian gangs as terrorist groups.

events.title: Nearly 5,000 Killed in Nine Months of 2025
events.detail: The UN reported nearly 5,000 people killed in Haiti in the first nine months of 2025, on pace to far exceed the 2024 toll. Violence had spread beyond Port-au-Prince into Artibonite, Centre, and Northwest departments. Gangs were moving along northern routes toward the Dominican Republic border. The June-July figure of 272 active IDP sites housed over 210,000 people in dire conditions.

events.title: Chérizier Indicted in US; $5M Bounty Offered
events.detail: The US Department of Justice indicted Jimmy 'Barbecue' Chérizier on conspiracy charges for evading US sanctions, and the State Department offered a $5 million reward for information leading to his arrest or conviction. Chérizier, who had survived unconfirmed reports of his death in March 2025, released videos denying the reports and vowing to continue fighting. He threatened attacks on the prime minister's office.

events.title: Laurent Saint-Cyr Takes TPC Presidency
events.detail: Laurent Saint-Cyr assumed the rotating presidency of the Transitional Presidential Council on August 7, 2025, succeeding Fritz Jean in the six-month rotation. Saint-Cyr's mandate was focused on organizing elections, then scheduled for August 30, 2026. He later refused to sign a motion by TPC factions to remove PM Fils-Aimé in January 2026, preventing a political crisis.

events.title: 90% of Port-au-Prince Under Gang Control
events.detail: UN reports confirmed that armed gangs controlled approximately 90% of Port-au-Prince by October 2025, up from 80% at the start of 2024. Over 200 gangs operated nationwide, with 95 active in the capital. The MSS, with fewer than 1,000 personnel, was deemed 'unfit for purpose' by independent analysts who noted the mission had received only 30% of its required equipment by June 2025.

events.title: UN Resolution 2793 Authorizes Gang Suppression Force
events.detail: The UN Security Council adopted Resolution 2793, authorizing the transition from the MSS to a new Gang Suppression Force (GSF) with a target of 5,500 personnel—more than double the MSS authorization. The resolution was co-sponsored by the US and Panama. Jack Christofides was named as GSF Special Representative in December 2025. A UN Support Office in Haiti (UNSOH) was established to provide logistics, fuel, medical, and accommodation support.

events.title: Over 8,100 Killed in Eleven Months of 2025
events.detail: By November 2025, UN figures showed over 8,100 people killed in Haiti in the first eleven months—surpassing the full-year 2024 total of 5,600. Violence had spread into previously calm northern, northwestern, and central departments. PAHO upgraded Haiti to a Grade 3 humanitarian crisis, the UN's highest emergency level. Over 6 million Haitians required humanitarian assistance.

events.title: TPC Mandate Expires; Fils-Aimé Assumes Sole Executive Power
events.detail: The Transitional Presidential Council's mandate expired on February 7, 2026, transferring executive authority to PM Alix Didier Fils-Aimé. Days earlier, TPC factions had attempted unsuccessfully to remove Fils-Aimé; TPC President Saint-Cyr refused to countersign the removal motion. On February 23, 2026, a 'National Pact for Stability' was signed by political parties and civil society, backing Fils-Aimé as the transitional authority pending August 2026 elections.

events.title: General Elections Planned for August 2026
events.detail: Haiti's Provisional Electoral Council (CEP) has scheduled elections for August 30, 2026 (first round) and December 6, 2026 (second round and local elections) — planned as Haiti's first general elections since 2016. Over 320 political parties have registered. Voter registration is planned for April–June 2026. Analysts warn that 23 communes remain under gang control, making voting physically impossible without a dramatic security improvement by the GSF. The elections remain subject to security conditions and logistical readiness.
